summary: A strategically located Gulf sultanate guarding the Strait of Hormuz, known for quiet diplomacy and neutrality — often the back-channel mediator between Iran, the West, and Arab states.

history.detail: Oman becomes an independent maritime power with an East African empire.
Omani empire stretches down the Swahili coast; controls the clove and slave trade.
Palace coup against his father; begins modernising an isolated, undeveloped country.
British and Iranian-backed forces defeat a Marxist insurgency in the south.
Limited unrest met with jobs and reforms rather than crackdown.
Haitham bin Tariq succeeds him after a 50-year reign; continuity maintained.

key_doctrines: Balancing Iran and the Gulf states

finance.currency_role: Omani rial (OMR); pegged to the US dollar
leverage_tools: Control of the Strait of Hormuz approaches

tech_startups.vc_ecosystem: Small but growing under Vision 2040 diversification
gov_tech_links: Ministry of Transport, Communications and IT

resources.oil_gas: Oil and gas ~30% of GDP; not an OPEC member but coordinates with it
resources.water: Scarce; reliant on desalination and ancient falaj irrigation
resources.agriculture: Dates, limes, fishing along a long coastline

follow_the_money.detail: Oil and LNG fund the state budget; vulnerable to price swings
Major Chinese and Gulf investment in a deepwater logistics and refining hub

theories_disputed.detail: Oman secretly hosted early US-Iran talks that led to the 2015 JCPOA.

connections.detail: Trusted mediator; shares control of the Strait of Hormuz
Long defence ties; UK base access at Duqm
GCC member but pursues an independent line
